title: rref
summary: Compute reduced row echelon form and MATLAB-style pivot column indices.

gpu_support.notes: Invokes the acceleration provider's rref hook when available; the current WGPU backend gathers to the host, runs the shared elimination implementation, and re-uploads R and pivcol to keep downstream residency intact.

description: `rref(A)` returns the reduced row echelon form of matrix `A`. `[R, pivcol] = rref(A)` also returns the 1-based pivot column indices as a row vector.
behaviors: Supports real numeric, logical, integer, scalar, vector, matrix, and complex matrix inputs. Logical and integer inputs are promoted to double precision before elimination.
The optional second argument `rref(A, tol)` overrides the pivot tolerance. Tolerance must be a finite, non-negative real scalar.
When tolerance is omitted, RunMat uses MATLAB-compatible scaling: `max(size(A)) * eps(norm(A, inf))`.
Pivot search proceeds left to right and chooses the largest-magnitude entry in the remaining rows of the active column.
Pivot column indices in `pivcol` are MATLAB-style 1-based double values. If no pivots are found, `pivcol` has shape `1 x 0`.
Empty matrices and all-zero matrices return unchanged matrix-shaped output with no pivot columns.
Complex inputs use complex division and magnitudes during pivot selection and elimination.

faqs.question: How is the default tolerance chosen?
faqs.answer: RunMat follows MATLAB's dense-matrix rule: `max(size(A)) * eps(norm(A, inf))`. Larger or more highly scaled matrices therefore receive a larger pivot cutoff.

faqs.question: What shape is `pivcol`?
faqs.answer: `pivcol` is always a row vector of 1-based double indices. No pivots returns an empty row vector with shape `1 x 0`.

faqs.question: Does `rref` support complex matrices?
faqs.answer: Yes. Complex values use magnitude-based pivoting and complex arithmetic for row normalization and elimination.

faqs.question: Will calling `rref` move GPU data back to the CPU?
faqs.answer: Only when the active provider lacks a native implementation. The current WGPU backend gathers, computes on the host, and re-uploads both outputs so later GPU work can continue with resident tensors.

gpu_residency: RunMat first offers `rref` to the active acceleration provider through the `rref` hook. Providers without a native implementation gather the input, run the shared host RREF algorithm, and upload both `R` and `pivcol` so downstream GPU kernels can continue without an explicit user-managed transfer.
gpu_behavior: The current WGPU backend implements `rref` as a provider hook backed by host fallback and re-upload. The builtin is not fused into elementwise kernels; producers flush before `rref` runs, and both outputs are new resident GPU handles when the provider path is active.
